have to buy slips of fruit trees for
his orchard and bushes for his small

If ne has not the cash, he will have

From his profits the settler must
pay back to the government his
farms proportionate share of the
cost of the irrigation works, these
payments to be in annual INSTALL
ments spread over a period of 10
years. at the end of that time, If he
can show that he has reclaimed a.
least one-half of the total irIisa BILE
area of his entry for agricultural
purposes, the land and the water
privileges are his forever.


The first payment for his water
rights will not be due until he has
had chance to raise one crop. Then
there will be no trouble to pay the
water right payment from this crop.


The settler who fails in two annual
pay ll0litS lOl Vic water ilSnlS Io1
feits the payments which he has
made and the land which he has en-
tered. 1t will be opened for entry to
some other settler


The water right payment on one
of these farms will be about sos a
year, or an annual charge for 10
years of $3.50 an acre. Thus at the
end of the 10 years the settler will
have paid $20S0. plus whatever he in
vests in labor and improvements. No
interest is charged


The value of one of these farm
homesteads, at the end of the 10-year
period, judging from the value of
other irrigated farms in the west,
should not be less than $4,000, and
will run from that up to $4,000 and
perhaps more. Some irrigation fruit
farms are rated at $3.50 an acre
There is never any crop failure due
to lack of rain on an irrigated farm
Crops may be depended on.


The farms have a soil adapted to
| berries, wheat. oats, potatoes, sugar
| beets and alfalfa.


The government has particular
| interest in this first of its irrigation
| projects, and will try to see that thu


best kind of settlers have a chance t.
b. take up the lands.


| And here is word of warning
! Beware of fake irrigation schemes


which are now trying to masquerade
| in the guise of government irriga
tion"--and don't take the information
| of railroad circulars too literally. !


lot of truth may be learned from LAM
agents for railroads, but il is safe


to direct inquiries to George Blsn

! tract for the mere Spectator. TIL
settler must work his farm; he ca
not take up more than one homestead
the limit of which has been place
at so acres; he must be prepared t


build his house and stay.


Settlers have already begun takin
